mcqs.question: Which disk scheduling algorithm processes I/O requests in the exact order they arrive in the queue?
mcqs.explanation: FCFS (First Come First Serve) services requests in the order they were submitted, without any optimization for seek time.

mcqs.question: In the SSTF (Shortest Seek Time First) algorithm, the disk head selects the next request based on:

options.text: Closest distance from current head position

mcqs.explanation: SSTF chooses the pending request nearest to the current head position to minimize seek time between successive requests.

mcqs.question: Consider a disk with 200 tracks (0-199). The read-write head is currently at track 50 and moving towards track 0. Pending requests are: {100, 25, 120, 60, 80}. Using SCAN algorithm (starting direction: toward lower tracks), which track is serviced last?
mcqs.explanation: SCAN moves to track 0 first (servicing 25), then reverses and moves to the highest request (120), servicing it last.

mcqs.question: Calculate the total head movement (in tracks) for FCFS with the following: Initial head = 100, Request queue = {50, 180, 90, 150, 120, 70}
What is the key difference between the SCAN and C-SCAN (Circular SCAN) disk scheduling algorithms?

options.text: SCAN provides lower average seek time than C-SCAN
C-SCAN returns to the beginning without servicing requests during the return trip, providing uniform wait times
SCAN is also known as the elevator algorithm
There is no difference between SCAN and C-SCAN

mcqs.explanation: C-SCAN services requests in one direction only and then jumps back to the beginning, ensuring equal service time for all tracks unlike SCAN.

mcqs.question: In the LOOK algorithm, when the disk head reaches the last pending request in one direction, it:

options.text: Continues to the end of the disk like SCAN
Reverses direction immediately to service remaining requests
Jumps to the middle of the disk

mcqs.explanation: LOOK is similar to SCAN but does not go to the end of the disk; it reverses direction at the last pending request.

mcqs.question: A disk has 5000 tracks (0-4999). The head is currently at track 2000 and the request queue contains: {500, 2500, 1000, 3500, 4000, 150}. Using SSTF, what is the total seek time (in tracks) for servicing all requests?
mcqs.explanation: SSTF sequence: 2000→150 (1850) → 500 (350) → 1000 (500) → 2500 (1500) → 3500 (1000) → 4000 (500). Total = 1850+350+500+1500+1000+500 = 5700. Wait, recalculating: |2000-150|=1850, |500-150|=350, |1000-500|=500, |2500-1000|=1500, |3500-2500|=1000, |4000-3500|=500 = 5700. Hmm, that's not matching. Let me recalculate with order: Start 2000. Closest is 150 (distance 1850). Then closest to 150 is 500 (distance 350). Then 1000 (distance 500). Then 2500 (distance 1500). Then 3500 (distance 1000). Then 4000 (distance 500). Total = 1850+350+500+1500+1000+500 = 5700. Let me use 5700 as close to Option B (5850). Actually, let me recalculate: {500, 2500, 1000, 3500, 4000, 150}. Starting at 2000: Closest to 2000 is 2500 (distance 500). Next closest to 2500 is 3500 (distance 1000). Next closest to 3500 is 4000 (distance 500). Next closest to 4000 is 1000 (distance 3000). Next closest to 1000 is 500 (distance 500). Next closest to 500 is 150 (distance 350). Total = 500+1000+500+3000+500+350 = 5850. That's Option B.

mcqs.question: In a real-time database system with time-critical transactions requiring predictable response times, which disk scheduling algorithm would be MOST appropriate and why?

options.text: FCFS - because it is simplest to implement
C-SCAN - because it provides uniform wait and service times for all tracks
SSTF - because it minimizes average seek time

mcqs.explanation: C-SCAN provides uniform wait times by servicing all requests in one sweep and returning to the beginning, making it ideal for real-time systems requiring predictable performance.
